docs.title: About
docs.text:
A repository of examples and inspiration for extending the users toolbox when writing unittests.

While the examples are written in Python, the concepts are applicable to any language that supports unit testing.

For people that are more into Python, some examples are implemented with both Pythons built in unittest framework as well as the third party pytest framework to show the differences and similarities between the two.

All examples are built using MkDocs and can be found here.

docs.title: What has been implemented so far
docs.text:
• [x] Assertion
  • [x] Test structure methodology
  • [x] Given, When, Then
  • [x] Arrange, Act, Assert
  • [x] Mocking
  • [x] Variables
  • [x] Functions
  • [x] Classes
  • [x] File input
  • [x] Directory structure
  • [x] API responses
  • [x] Environment variables
  • [x] Fixtures
  • [x] Parametrization
  • [x] Markers
  • [ ] Exceptions
  • [ ] Capturing Output
  • [ ] Coverage reporting
  • [ ] Plugins
  • [ ] Logging

docs.title: tests.test_assertion.TestAssertion
docs.text: Assertion is one of the fundamental concepts in testing, used to validate the expected result of a test. It is used to compare the actual result with the expected result. If the actual result matches the expected result, the test case passes; otherwise, it fails.

docs.title: test_assertion_pass()
docs.text:
In this test case, the assertion is True, so the test case passes.

Source code in tests/test_assertion.py 
def test_assertion_pass(self):
    """
    In this test case, the assertion is True, so the test case passes.
    """
    assert 1 == 1

docs.title: test_assertion_fail()
docs.text:
In this test case, the assertion is False, so the test case fails. The 'xfail' marker indicates that this test is expected to fail.

Source code in tests/test_assertion.py 
@pytest.mark.xfail(reason="This test is expected to fail as a demonstration.")
def test_assertion_fail(self):
    """
    In this test case, the assertion is False, so the test case fails.
    The 'xfail' marker indicates that this test is expected to fail.
    """
    assert 1 == 2

docs.title: test_assertion_with_message()
docs.text:
You can also provide a message to the assertion to make it more informative. Note: The message is displayed only when the assertion fails.

Source code in tests/test_assertion.py 
@pytest.mark.xfail(reason="This test is expected to fail as a demonstration.")
def test_assertion_with_message(self):
    """
    You can also provide a message to the assertion to make it more informative.
    Note: The message is displayed only when the assertion fails.
    """
    assert 1 == 2, "1 is not equal to 2"

docs.title: test_list_equality()
docs.text:
This test checks if two lists are equal.

Source code in tests/test_assertion.py 
def test_list_equality(self):
    """
    This test checks if two lists are equal.
    """
    list1 = [1, 2, 3]
    list2 = [1, 2, 3]
    assert list1 == list2, "The two lists should be equal"

docs.title: test_string_contains()
docs.text:
This test checks if a string contains a substring.

Source code in tests/test_assertion.py 
def test_string_contains(self):
    """
    This test checks if a string contains a substring.
    """
    string = "Hello, world!"
    substring = "world"
    assert substring in string, f"The string should contain the substring '{substring}'"

docs.title: test_dict_key_presence()
docs.text:
This test checks if a key is present in a dictionary.

Source code in tests/test_assertion.py 
def test_dict_key_presence(self):
    """
    This test checks if a key is present in a dictionary.
    """
    my_dict = {"key1": "value1", "key2": "value2"}
    assert "key1" in my_dict, "The key 'key1' should be present in the dictionary"

docs.title: test_none_check()
docs.text:
This test checks if a variable is None.

Source code in tests/test_assertion.py 
def test_none_check(self):
    """
    This test checks if a variable is None.
    """
    var = None
    assert var is None, "The variable should be None"

docs.title: test_boolean_expression()
docs.text:
This test checks the truthiness of a boolean expression.

Source code in tests/test_assertion.py 
def test_boolean_expression(self):
    """
    This test checks the truthiness of a boolean expression.
    """
    condition = (1 + 1 == 2)
    assert condition, "The boolean expression should be True"

docs.title: test_approximate_equality()
docs.text:
This test checks if two floating-point numbers are approximately equal.

Source code in tests/test_assertion.py 
def test_approximate_equality(self):
    """
    This test checks if two floating-point numbers are approximately equal.
    """
    num1 = 0.1 + 0.2
    num2 = 0.3
    assert num1 == pytest.approx(num2), "The two numbers should be approximately equal"

docs.title: tests.test_fixtures.TestFixtures
docs.text:
Fixtures in Pytest provide a way to initialize resources and to provide these resources to the test functions.

It's a great way to define reusable data or setup code that can be used across multiple tests.

Note

Fixtures usually does not go into a class, but for demonstration purposes, we are using a class here to group the fixtures together.

docs.title: simple_data()
docs.text:
This fixture provides a simple dataset for testing purposes.

Source code in tests/test_fixtures.py 
@pytest.fixture
def simple_data(self):
    """
    This fixture provides a simple dataset for testing purposes.
    """
    return {"key": "value", "number": 42}

docs.title: complex_data()
docs.text:
This fixture provides a more complex dataset.

Source code in tests/test_fixtures.py 
@pytest.fixture
def complex_data(self):
    """
    This fixture provides a more complex dataset.
    """
    data = {
        "users": [
            {"id": 1, "name": "Alice"},
            {"id": 2, "name": "Bob"},
        ],
        "settings": {
            "theme": "dark",
            "notifications": True
        }
    }
    return data

docs.title: database_connection()
docs.text:
This fixture mocks a database connection. It is set to module scope so it will be setup once per module and shared between tests.

Source code in tests/test_fixtures.py 
@pytest.fixture(scope='module')
def database_connection(self):
    """
    This fixture mocks a database connection. It is set to module
    scope so it will be setup once per module and shared between tests.
    """
    connection = "database_connection"
    yield connection
    # Tear down
    connection = None
    print("Closed database connection")

docs.title: app_config()
docs.text:
This fixture provides application configuration. It is set to session scope so it will be setup once per test session.

Source code in tests/test_fixtures.py 
@pytest.fixture(scope='session')
def app_config(self):
    """
    This fixture provides application configuration.
    It is set to session scope so it will be setup once per test session.
    """
    config = {
        "version": "1.0.0",
        "name": "Sample Application"
    }
    yield config

docs.title: temp_file(tmp_path)
docs.text:
This fixture creates a temporary file for testing.

Source code in tests/test_fixtures.py 
@pytest.fixture
def temp_file(self, tmp_path):
    """
    This fixture creates a temporary file for testing.
    """
    file = tmp_path / "temp_file.txt"
    file.write_text("Temporary file content")
    return file

docs.title: mock_environment_variable(monkeypatch)
docs.text:
This fixture mocks an environment variable using the monkeypatch plugin from pytest.

Source code in tests/test_fixtures.py 
@pytest.fixture
def mock_environment_variable(self, monkeypatch):
    """
    This fixture mocks an environment variable using the monkeypatch plugin from pytest.
    """
    monkeypatch.setenv("ENV_VAR", "mocked_value")
    yield
    monkeypatch.undo()

docs.title: tests.test_fixtures.TestUsingFixtures

Bases: TestFixtures

This class demonstrates the usage of the fixtures defined in the TestFixtures class.

docs.title: test_simple_data_fixture(simple_data)
docs.text:
This test uses the simple_data fixture.

Source code in tests/test_fixtures.py 
def test_simple_data_fixture(self, simple_data):
    """
    This test uses the simple_data fixture.
    """
    assert simple_data["key"] == "value"
    assert simple_data["number"] == 42

docs.title: test_complex_data_fixture(complex_data)
docs.text:
This test uses the complex_data fixture.

Source code in tests/test_fixtures.py 
def test_complex_data_fixture(self, complex_data):
    """
    This test uses the complex_data fixture.
    """
    users = complex_data["users"]
    assert isinstance(users, list)
    assert len(users) == 2
    assert users[0]["name"] == "Alice"
    assert complex_data["settings"]["theme"] == "dark"

docs.title: test_mock_environment_variable_fixture(mock_environment_variable)

docs.title: test_combined_fixtures(simple_data, complex_data, app_config)
docs.text:
This test uses multiple fixtures to verify combined use.

Source code in tests/test_fixtures.py 
def test_combined_fixtures(self, simple_data, complex_data, app_config):
    """
    This test uses multiple fixtures to verify combined use.
    """
    assert simple_data["key"] == "value"
    assert len(complex_data["users"]) == 2
    assert app_config["version"] == "1.0.0"

docs.title: tests.test_markers.TestMarkers
docs.text:
This class demonstrates the usage of various markers in pytest.

Custom Markers: Custom markers allow you to categorize tests for different purposes.

To define custom markers, you should register them in a pyproject.toml file. Create a pyproject.toml file in your project root with the following content:

[tool.pytest.ini_options] markers = [ "slow: marks tests as slow (deselect with '-m "not slow"')", "fast: marks tests as fast", "regression: marks tests for regression testing" ]

Usage: - Run tests marked as slow: pytest -m slow - Run tests excluding the slow ones: pytest -m "not slow" - Run tests marked for regression: pytest -m regression

docs.title: test_skip_example()
docs.text:
This test is skipped and will not be run.

Source code in tests/test_markers.py 
@pytest.mark.skip(reason="Skipping this test for demonstration purposes")
def test_skip_example(self):
    """
    This test is skipped and will not be run.
    """
    assert 1 == 1

docs.title: test_conditional_skip()
docs.text:
This test is conditionally skipped based on some condition.

Source code in tests/test_markers.py 
@pytest.mark.skipif(True, reason="This test is conditionally skipped")
def test_conditional_skip(self):
    """
    This test is conditionally skipped based on some condition.
    """
    assert True

docs.title: test_expected_fail()
docs.text:
This test is expected to fail.

Source code in tests/test_markers.py 
@pytest.mark.xfail(reason="This test is expected to fail")
def test_expected_fail(self):
    """
    This test is expected to fail.
    """
    assert 1 == 2

docs.title: test_parametrized(input_data, expected)
docs.text:
This test uses the parametrize marker to test multiple inputs.

You can read more about parametrized tests in the parametrization section.

Source code in tests/test_markers.py 
@pytest.mark.parametrize("input_data, expected", [
    (1, 2),
    (2, 3),
    (3, 4),
])
def test_parametrized(self, input_data, expected):
    """
    This test uses the parametrize marker to test multiple inputs.

    You can read more about parametrized tests in the
    [parametrization](parametrization.md#TestParametrization) section.
    """
    assert input_data + 1 == expected

docs.title: tests.test_mocking.TestMockingVariables
docs.text: Mocking is a technique used in unit testing to replace real objects with mock objects. This is useful when you want to isolate the code being tested from external dependencies.

docs.title: test_mock_variable_unittest()
docs.text:
This test demonstrates mocking a variable with Pythons built-in unittest.mock library.

Source code in tests/test_mocking.py 
def test_mock_variable_unittest(self):
    """
    This test demonstrates mocking a variable with Pythons
    built-in unittest.mock library.
    """
    with mock.patch("tests.test_mocking.VARIABLE", "mocked_value"):
        assert VARIABLE == "mocked_value"

docs.title: test_mock_variable_pytest(mocker)
docs.text:
This test demonstrates mocking a variable using the pytest-mock library.

Source code in tests/test_mocking.py 
def test_mock_variable_pytest(self, mocker):
    """
    This test demonstrates mocking a variable using the pytest-mock library.
    """
    mocker.patch("tests.test_mocking.VARIABLE", "mocked_value")
    assert VARIABLE == "mocked_value"

docs.title: tests.test_mocking.TestMockingFunctions
docs.text: This class demonstrates how to mock functions.

docs.title: test_mock_function_unittest()
docs.text:
This test demonstrates mocking a function with Pythons built-in unittest.mock library.

Source code in tests/test_mocking.py 
def test_mock_function_unittest(self):
    """
    This test demonstrates mocking a function with Pythons
    built-in unittest.mock library.
    """
    def mock_function():
        return "mocked result"
    with mock.patch("tests.test_mocking.function_to_mock", mock_function):
        assert function_to_mock() == "mocked result"

docs.title: test_mock_function_unittest_assert_called_with()
docs.text:
This test demonstrates how to assert that a mocked function was called with specific arguments.

Note that we do not use a separate function here. Because then we would have replaced the mocked function with our function, which do not have the assert_called_with method.

Source code in tests/test_mocking.py 
def test_mock_function_unittest_assert_called_with(self):
    """
    This test demonstrates how to assert that a mocked function
    was called with specific arguments.

    Note that we do not use a separate function here. Because then we would have
    replaced the mocked function with our function, which do not have the
    assert_called_with method.
    """

    with mock.patch("tests.test_mocking.function_to_mock") as mocked_function:
        function_to_mock("mocked result")
        mocked_function.assert_called_with("mocked result")

docs.title: test_mock_function_pytest(mocker)
docs.text:
This test demonstrates mocking a function using the pytest-mock library.

Note that this do not use a context manager like the unittest.mock library.

Source code in tests/test_mocking.py 
def test_mock_function_pytest(self, mocker):
    """
    This test demonstrates mocking a function using the pytest-mock library.

    Note that this do not use a context manager like the unittest.mock library.
    """
    mocker.patch("tests.test_mocking.function_to_mock", return_value="mocked result")
    assert function_to_mock() == "mocked result"

docs.title: test_mock_function_pytest_assert_called_with(mocker)
docs.text:
This test demonstrates how to assert that a mocked function was called with specific arguments using the pytest-mock library.

Note that this do not use a context manager like the unittest.mock library.

Source code in tests/test_mocking.py 
def test_mock_function_pytest_assert_called_with(self, mocker):
    """
    This test demonstrates how to assert that a mocked function
    was called with specific arguments using the pytest-mock library.

    Note that this do not use a context manager like the unittest.mock library.
    """
    mocked_function = mocker.patch("tests.test_mocking.function_to_mock")
    function_to_mock("mocked result")
    mocked_function.assert_called_with("mocked result")

docs.title: tests.test_mocking.TestMockingClasses
docs.text: This class demonstrates how to mock classes, methods, and class variables.

docs.title: test_mock_class_variable_unittest()
docs.text:
This test demonstrates mocking a class variable using the unittest.mock library.

Source code in tests/test_mocking.py 
def test_mock_class_variable_unittest(self):
    """
    This test demonstrates mocking a class variable using the
    unittest.mock library.
    """
    class MockClass:
        variable = "mocked variable"
    with mock.patch("tests.test_mocking.ClassToMock", MockClass):
        instance = ClassToMock()
        assert instance.variable == "mocked variable"

docs.title: test_mock_class_variable_pytest(mocker)
docs.text:
This test demonstrates mocking a class variable using the pytest-mock library.

Source code in tests/test_mocking.py 
def test_mock_class_variable_pytest(self, mocker):
    """
    This test demonstrates mocking a class variable using the pytest-mock library.
    """
    mocker.patch("tests.test_mocking.ClassToMock.variable", "mocked variable")
    instance = ClassToMock()
    assert instance.variable == "mocked variable"

docs.title: test_mock_class_method_unittest()
docs.text:
This test demonstrates mocking a class method using the unittest.mock library.

Source code in tests/test_mocking.py 
def test_mock_class_method_unittest(self):
    """
    This test demonstrates mocking a class method using the
    unittest.mock library.
    """
    class MockClass:
        def method(self):
            return "mocked method result"
    with mock.patch("tests.test_mocking.ClassToMock", MockClass):
        instance = ClassToMock()
        assert instance.method() == "mocked method result"

    """
    mocker.patch("tests.test_mocking.ClassToMock.method", return_value="mocked method result")

docs.title: test_mock_file_unittest()
docs.text:
This test demonstrates mocking file input using the unittest.mock library.

Source code in tests/test_mocking.py 
def test_mock_file_unittest(self):
    """
    This test demonstrates mocking file input using the unittest.mock library.
    """
    mock_open = mock.mock_open(read_data="mocked file content")
    with mock.patch("builtins.open", mock_open):
        with open("dummy_file.txt") as f:
            assert f.read() == "mocked file content"

docs.title: test_mock_file_pytest(mocker)
docs.text:
This test demonstrates mocking file input using the pytest-mock library.

Source code in tests/test_mocking.py 
def test_mock_file_pytest(self, mocker):
    """
    This test demonstrates mocking file input using the pytest-mock library.
    """
    mock_open = mocker.mock_open(read_data="mocked file content")
    mocker.patch("builtins.open", mock_open)

    with open("dummy_file.txt") as f:
        assert f.read() == "mocked file content"

docs.title: test_mock_directory_unittest()
docs.text:
This test demonstrates mocking directory structure with files using the unittest.mock library.

Source code in tests/test_mocking.py 
def test_mock_directory_unittest(self):
    """
    This test demonstrates mocking directory structure with files
    using the unittest.mock library.
    """
    with TemporaryDirectory() as d:
        with mock.patch("os.listdir", return_value=["file1.txt", "file2.txt"]):
            assert sorted(os.listdir(d)) == ["file1.txt",
                                             "file2.txt"]

docs.title: test_mock_directory_pytest(tmp_path)
docs.text:
This test demonstrates mocking directory structure with files using the tmp_path fixture from pytest.

Source code in tests/test_mocking.py 
def test_mock_directory_pytest(self, tmp_path):
    """
    This test demonstrates mocking directory structure with files
    using the tmp_path fixture from pytest.
    """
    d = tmp_path / "dummy_directory"
    d.mkdir()
    (d / "file1.txt").write_text("content1")
    (d / "file2.txt").write_text("content2")
    with mock.patch("os.listdir", return_value=sorted([p.name for p in d.iterdir()])):
        assert sorted(os.listdir(d)) == ["file1.txt", "file2.txt"]

docs.title: tests.test_mocking.TestMockingApi
docs.text: This class demonstrates how to mock API responses.

docs.title: test_mock_api_response_unittest()
docs.text:
This test demonstrates mocking an API response.

Source code in tests/test_mocking.py 
def test_mock_api_response_unittest(self):
    """
    This test demonstrates mocking an API response.
    """
    mock_response = mock.Mock()
    mock_response.status_code = 200
    mock_response.json.return_value = {"key": "value"}
    with mock.patch("requests.get", return_value=mock_response):
        response = requests.get("https://api.example.com/data")
        assert response.status_code == 200
        assert response.json() == {"key": "value"}

docs.title: test_mock_api_response_pytest(mocker)
docs.text:
This test demonstrates mocking an API response using the pytest-mock library.

Source code in tests/test_mocking.py 
def test_mock_api_response_pytest(self, mocker):
    """
    This test demonstrates mocking an API response using the pytest-mock library.
    """
    mock_response = mocker.Mock()
    mock_response.status_code = 200
    mock_response.json.return_value = {"key": "value"}
    mocker.patch("requests.get", return_value=mock_response)
    response = requests.get("https://api.example.com/data")
    assert response.status_code == 200
    assert response.json() == {"key": "value"}

docs.title: tests.test_parametrization.TestParametrization
docs.text: This class demonstrates the usage of parameterized tests in pytest. Parameterized tests allow you to define multiple sets of arguments for a single test function.

docs.title: test_add_one(input_data, expected)
docs.text:
This test checks if adding 1 to the input_data produces the expected result.

Source code in tests/test_parametrization.py 
@pytest.mark.parametrize("input_data, expected", [
    (1, 2),
    (2, 3),
    (3, 4),
])
def test_add_one(self, input_data, expected):
    """
    This test checks if adding 1 to the input_data produces the expected result.
    """
    assert input_data + 1 == expected

docs.title: test_string_contains(string, substring, expected)
docs.text:
This test checks if a substring is present in the main string.

Source code in tests/test_parametrization.py 
@pytest.mark.parametrize("string, substring, expected", [
    ("Hello, world!", "world", True),
    ("Hello, world!", "Python", False),
    ("Pytest is great", "great", True),
])
def test_string_contains(self, string, substring, expected):
    """
    This test checks if a substring is present in the main string.
    """
    assert (substring in string) == expected

docs.title: test_list_equality(list1, list2, expected)
docs.text:
This test checks if two lists are equal.

Source code in tests/test_parametrization.py 
@pytest.mark.parametrize("list1, list2, expected", [
    ([1, 2, 3], [1, 2, 3], True),
    ([1, 2], [2, 1], False),
    ([], [], True),
])
def test_list_equality(self, list1, list2, expected):
    """
    This test checks if two lists are equal.
    """
    assert (list1 == list2) == expected

docs.title: test_division(dividend, divisor, expected)
docs.text:
This test checks if division produces the expected result.

Source code in tests/test_parametrization.py 
@pytest.mark.parametrize("dividend, divisor, expected", [
    (10, 2, 5),
    (9, 3, 3),
    (5, 2, 2.5),
])
def test_division(self, dividend, divisor, expected):
    """
    This test checks if division produces the expected result.
    """
    assert dividend / divisor == expected

docs.title: test_power(base, exponent, expected)
docs.text:
This test checks if raising a base to an exponent gives the expected result.

Source code in tests/test_parametrization.py 
@pytest.mark.parametrize("base, exponent, expected", [
    (2, 3, 8),
    (3, 2, 9),
    (5, 0, 1),
])
def test_power(self, base, exponent, expected):
    """
    This test checks if raising a base to an exponent gives the expected result.
    """
    assert base ** exponent == expected

docs.title: test_dict_values(person, request_param, expected_value)
docs.text:
This test checks if the dictionary returns the expected value for a given key.

Source code in tests/test_parametrization.py 
@pytest.mark.parametrize("person, request_param, expected_value", [
    ({"name": "Alice", "age": 30}, "name", "Alice"),
    ({"name": "Bob", "age": 25}, "age", 25),
    ({"name": "Charlie", "age": 35}, "name", "Charlie"),
])
def test_dict_values(self, person, request_param, expected_value):
    """
    This test checks if the dictionary returns the expected value for a given key.
    """
    assert person[request_param] == expected_value

docs.title: test_sum_of_list(integer_list, target_sum, expected)
docs.text:
This test checks if the sum of the list equals the target sum.

Source code in tests/test_parametrization.py 
@pytest.mark.parametrize("integer_list, target_sum, expected", [
    ([1, 2, 3], 6, True),
    ([1, 2, 3], 7, False),
    ([1, 1, 1], 3, True),
])
def test_sum_of_list(self, integer_list, target_sum, expected):
    """
    This test checks if the sum of the list equals the target sum.
    """
    assert (sum(integer_list) == target_sum) == expected

docs.title: Test structure methodology
docs.text: This section will cover different methodologies for structuring tests. The goal is to make tests easier to read and understand, and to make it easier to write tests that are maintainable and scalable.

docs.title: Given, When, Then
docs.text:
The Given, When, Then (GWT) test structure is a way to organize tests in a way that makes them easier to read and understand. The structure is as follows:

  1. Given: Set up the initial state of the system under test (SUT).
  2. When: Perform the action that you want to test.
  3. Then: Check that the expected result has occurred.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
def test_addition():
    # Given
    x = 1
    y = 2

    # When
    result = x + y

    # Then
    assert result == 3

docs.title: Arrange, Act, Assert
docs.text:
The Arrange, Act, Assert (AAA) test structure is another way to organize tests. The structure is as follows:

  1. Arrange: Set up the initial state of the system under test (SUT).
  2. Act: Perform the action that you want to test.
  3. Assert: Check that the expected result has occurred.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
def test_addition():
    # Arrange
    x = 1
    y = 2

    # Act
    result = x + y

    # Assert
    assert result == 3

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: Both the GWT and AAA test structures are useful ways to organize tests. The choice of which structure to use will depend on personal preference and the specific requirements of the test. The most important thing is to be consistent in a project and to make sure that tests are easy to read and understand.
